items.stem: A bond has a modified duration of 8. Yields fall by 0.25 percentage points. Approximately what happens to its price?

options.explanation: Right: −8 × (−0.25%) ≈ +2%. Modified duration converts the yield move into the price move — sign flipped, size scaled.
Direction error — yields FELL, so the price rises. The see-saw still runs everything; duration only sizes the move.
That's the move for a full one-point change in yields. Scale by the actual move: 8 × 0.25 = 2, not 8.

options.text: Nothing — duration only matters if you hold to maturity
options.explanation: Modified duration is about NOW: it measures how today's price responds to today's yield moves. Holding to maturity is a separate question.

items.stem: Two bonds mature in exactly 10 years. Bond A pays an 8% coupon; Bond B pays a 2% coupon. Which price is more sensitive to a rise in yields?

options.text: Bond A — bigger coupons mean bigger exposure
options.explanation: Backwards: big coupons return value EARLY, which shields the price. Rate sensitivity lives in the far future, and Bond A has less of its value parked there.

options.text: Bond B — with a small coupon, most of its value arrives late, so its duration is higher
options.explanation: Correct. A low coupon back-loads the payoff, and back-loaded value is discounted hardest when yields rise. Same maturity, higher duration, bigger price swing.

options.text: Identical — same maturity means same rate risk
options.explanation: This is the duration-vs-maturity trap. The maturities match; the TIMING of the value does not. Sensitivity follows the timing, not the calendar.

options.text: Neither moves — coupons are fixed by contract
options.explanation: Fixed payments, moving prices — the foundational distinction of this path. Both prices move; the question is which moves more.
